identity.headline: Product Research @ Neomotion • Data Analyst @ IN22 Labs

currently_working_on: Building a risk-constrained reinforcement learning model for revenue management in B2C platforms.
Exploring whether India is moving towards an experience economy through trends in cafés, concerts, and urban leisure.
Studying GenAI usage patterns among 391 engineering students in Chennai to understand adoption behavior and engagement intensity.

featured_project.title: Spendception: Designing Mindful Spending in UPI
featured_project.description: Explores how real-time behavioral nudges during payments can reduce impulsive spending and improve financial awareness.

paragraphs: I’m an engineering student graduating this year, and most of my interest in product comes from noticing everyday problems and not being able to stop thinking about them. During placements I found it surprisingly difficult to connect with the right alumni mentors, which pushed me to start working on a matchmaking style platform that connects students with alumni in industry, something like a "Tinder for mentorship."

I’m also curious about the systems behind everyday experiences and why certain products suddenly become popular among young people. Watching ride hailing prices surge near my college pushed me to explore how platforms decide prices and begin building a reinforcement learning model around revenue management.
During my internships I explored problems from both data and product perspectives. At IN22 Labs I analyzed Tamil Nadu Roadways operational patterns and spending decisions.

At Neomotion I worked with the founders on product storytelling, user interviews, and turning real user experiences into case studies around NeoBolt and NeoFly. Outside internships, I enjoy exploring product ideas like a mindful spending concept that won the FinTech special track at K!Hacks.
about.product_philosophy: I believe great product decisions start with falling in love with the problem.

projects.title: Mentorship Matchmaking Platform
projects.description: Exploring a swipe-based mentorship discovery platform to help students quickly connect with relevant alumni mentors during placements.

detail.overview:
During campus placements, students often try to reach out to alumni for guidance on roles, interviews, and referrals.

However, discovering the right mentor is surprisingly difficult. Students rely on scattered LinkedIn searches, WhatsApp groups, or alumni lists.

This project explores a swipe-based mentorship discovery platform that helps students quickly discover relevant alumni mentors.
detail.problem:
Students preparing for placements struggle to connect with relevant alumni mentors who can guide them through interviews, roles, and referrals.

While alumni are often willing to help, there is no simple product that helps students quickly discover mentors based on company, role, or experience.

Existing discovery methods rely on manual searching through LinkedIn or informal networks, which leads to low response rates and missed opportunities.

How might we help students quickly discover and connect with the most relevant alumni mentors during placement preparation?
detail.my_role:
Defined the product problem and explored how mentorship discovery currently works during campus placements.

Conducted lightweight user research with students preparing for placements to understand how they currently reach out to alumni mentors.

Designed and validated a swipe-based discovery concept that helps students quickly explore relevant mentors without manual searching.
detail.approach:
I approached this problem using a lightweight product discovery process.

• Interviewed students preparing for placements to understand their current mentorship discovery patterns.
• Identified key friction points in discovering and reaching out to alumni mentors.
• Explored multiple discovery approaches before converging on a swipe-based exploration model.
• Designed a simple prototype to visualize and test the concept.
detail.decisions:
Swipe vs Search
Swipe-based exploration reduces cognitive load and allows faster discovery compared to traditional filter-heavy search interfaces.

Compatibility over Availability
Instead of listing all mentors, the concept focuses on surfacing the most relevant mentors first based on role, company, and experience.

Student-first discovery
The design prioritizes student discovery during placement preparation rather than building a complex two-sided browsing system.
detail.outcome:
The concept highlights how reducing mentor discovery friction can improve mentorship engagement during placements.

Students reported that a swipe-style discovery flow felt faster and less intimidating than manually reaching out to alumni on LinkedIn.

The prototype demonstrates how structured mentor discovery could make placement preparation more accessible.
detail.learnings:
Students rarely struggle with the willingness to ask for help , they struggle with discovering the right person to ask.

Reducing discovery friction can often create more impact than adding additional product features.

Simple interaction models like swipe exploration can sometimes outperform complex search systems during early discovery.

projects.title: Mindful Spending Behavior
projects.description: A fintech product concept exploring how behavioral nudges and contextual insights can improve spending awareness during digital payments.

detail.overview:
Digital payment systems like UPI have made transactions extremely convenient, but this convenience often reduces awareness of spending behavior.

Users frequently make multiple small payments throughout the day without realizing how these expenses accumulate.

This case study explores how contextual nudges and real-time financial insights can help users become more mindful of spending during the moment of payment.
detail.problem:
Digital payment platforms make spending fast and frictionless, which often leads to unconscious spending behavior.

Many users struggle to track frequent small transactions and only realize overspending after reviewing monthly summaries.

How might we design a product experience that encourages users to reflect on spending decisions before completing digital payments?
detail.my_role:
Conducted product discovery to analyze spending awareness challenges in digital payments.

Explored behavioral design principles to identify opportunities for nudges during payment interactions.

Designed product concepts and prototype interfaces demonstrating how contextual insights could be integrated into everyday spending experiences.
detail.approach:
The exploration followed a lightweight product discovery process.

• Investigated spending behavior patterns associated with digital payments.
• Conducted a survey to understand user awareness of micro-spending.
• Identified opportunities for behavioral nudges within payment flows.
• Prioritized features using product frameworks.
• Developed prototype interfaces illustrating spending awareness prompts and financial insights.
detail.decisions:
Nudge Instead of Restrict
The concept avoids blocking transactions and instead introduces behavioral nudges that encourage reflection before spending.

Awareness at the Moment of Payment
Traditional finance tools provide reports after spending occurs. This concept focuses on delivering insights during the payment flow.

Highlighting Micro-Spending Patterns
Frequent small payments often go unnoticed. The product surfaces these patterns to improve financial awareness.
detail.outcome:
The concept demonstrates how financial tools can move beyond passive expense tracking and provide real-time decision support.

By introducing contextual prompts and spending insights during payment interactions, the product encourages more mindful financial behavior without disrupting the user experience.

This approach highlights opportunities for fintech platforms to improve financial habits through better product design.
detail.learnings:
Many financial tools fail not because users lack spending data, but because they lack insights at the moment of financial decision-making.

Behavioral nudges delivered during transactions can influence spending behavior more effectively than traditional budgeting systems.

Products that support mindful financial decisions can create greater long-term user value than tools focused solely on expense tracking.

product_thinking.title: Making Carpooling Predictable and Trust-Based
product_thinking.summary: Transforms random ride pooling into a reliable, recurring commute through trusted user circles.

detail.problem_analysis: Ride pooling fails due to unpredictability, detours, and lack of trust among strangers.
detail.user_analysis: Students and professionals commuting daily on fixed routes with predictable schedules.
detail.pain_points: Unreliable ETA, safety concerns, random co-passengers, and inconsistent ride experience.
detail.proposed_solution: Create SmartPool Circles — small, recurring groups of verified commuters traveling together on fixed schedules.
detail.impact_metrics: Circle formation rate, ride reliability score, weekly rides per user, and retention within circles.

product_thinking.title: Turning Event Discovery into Social Attendance
product_thinking.summary: Adds a social layer to help users find companions and actually attend events they discover.

detail.problem_analysis: Users discover and save events but fail to attend due to lack of companionship.
detail.user_analysis: Young professionals and individuals with weak local social networks.
detail.pain_points: Fear of attending alone, lack of coordination tools, and no event-specific social matching.
detail.proposed_solution: Enable users to match with others attending the same event and form temporary social groups.
detail.impact_metrics: Event attendance rate, match success rate, user engagement, and repeat usage.

product_thinking.title: Solving Food Personalization Beyond Delivery
product_thinking.summary: A new category bridging food delivery and home cooking through personalized, in-home chef experiences.

detail.problem_analysis: Food delivery offers convenience but lacks personalization, health transparency, and control over ingredients.
detail.user_analysis: Urban professionals and families seeking healthy, customized meals without the effort of cooking.
detail.pain_points: No dietary customization, lack of trust in restaurant food, high cost of full-time cooks, and no middle-ground solution.
detail.proposed_solution: Introduce Chef@Home — a platform where users book verified chefs, customize meals, and get food cooked at home using fresh ingredients.
detail.impact_metrics: Chef rebooking rate, subscription conversion rate, monthly recurring sessions, and Instamart attachment rate.

internships.learnings: Identified gaps in budget-to-performance alignment across districts through data analysis
Translated real user experiences into product narratives used in CSR storytelling

hackathons.learnings:
🏆 FinTech Track Winner • Top 25 out of 1282 teams

Learned that influencing user behavior is more effective when integrated seamlessly into existing flows rather than adding extra steps.

Understood that reducing friction at the moment of decision-making drives better outcomes than post-action insights.

🥈 Runner-up among ~20+ teams

Learned how to design for multiple stakeholders (patients, pharmacists, providers) without increasing complexity in the workflow.

Realized that scalable healthcare solutions must balance accuracy, usability, and system integration.

Learned how real-time data (gas + motion sensing) can reduce decision latency in critical environments like worker safety.

Understood that in high-risk scenarios, the value of a product lies in how quickly and clearly it enables action.
